pricing.source: OpenAI API pricing, standard short-context rates, 2026-06-24

hardChecks.label: Citation source ids
hardChecks.expectation: Every cited source id exists in the source packet

hardChecks.label: Cost metadata
hardChecks.expectation: estimatedUsd is present when pricing is configured

hardChecks.label: Token metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Input, cached-input, and output token counts are coherent

hardChecks.label: Provider and model metadata
hardChecks.expectation: Provider, model, and prompt version are present

hardChecks.label: Judge input boundary
hardChecks.expectation: Judge prompt omits eval-only object keys and holdout tuning fields

hardChecks.label: Judge claim coverage
hardChecks.expectation: LLM judge returns exactly one judgment for every briefing claim

claimJudgments.claimText: Deeper traces increased median token usage (~+11%) but improved overall latency (~-6%) because fewer outputs needed manual reruns; gains came from fewer repeated attempts, not shorter single generations.
claimJudgments.explanation: S1 directly states token usage rose 11%, latency improved 6%, and the latency improvement came from fewer repeated attempts/manual reruns rather than shorter single generations.

claimJudgments.claimText: Finance will approve demo-quality runs up to the reference target cost budget if we explain how extra tokens reduce review time; exceeding budget requires smaller batch size, stricter retry caps, or more aggressive trace summarization.
claimJudgments.explanation: S2 says Finance approved demo-quality eval runs up to the reference target cost budget if the report explains how extra tokens reduce review time, and that exceeding the budget requires reducing batch size, capping retries, or summarizing traces more aggressively.

claimJudgments.claimText: Reviewers find long traces useful only when summarized next to the failed claim; raw provider payload dumps slow review due to search overhead.
claimJudgments.explanation: S3 directly supports both parts: reviewers valued traces when summarized/attached near the failed claim and said raw payloads slowed review because they had to search for relevant evidence.

claimJudgments.claimText: Live demo perceived as responsive when single briefings complete under ~10s and the UI shows per-case progress; stakeholders tolerate longer full runs if progress and artifact paths update continuously.
claimJudgments.explanation: S4 states single briefings felt live-demo safe under 10 seconds and that longer full eval runs were acceptable if current case, elapsed time/progress, and artifact paths updated continuously.

claimJudgments.claimText: Draft policy supports a small retry cap: allow one automatic retry for transient provider errors and require a manifest entry when retries change cost/latency; it rejects silent repeated generations.
claimJudgments.explanation: S5 directly says the draft proposes a small retry cap, allows one automatic retry for transient provider errors, requires manifest entries when retries change cost or latency, and rejects silent repeated generations.

recommendationJudgment.explanation: The recommendation directly answers how much tracing and retry behavior to keep: retain claim-level/full traces on disk but show short per-failed-claim summaries, and enforce one automatic retry only for transient provider errors with manifest logging. This is well supported by S3 and S6 for trace presentation, S5 for retry limits, S2 for budget constraints, and S4 for demo responsiveness. The open questions appropriately flag uncertainty about whether the current run exceeds the reference budget.

citationSupport.note: S1 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S2 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S3 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S4 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.
S5 appears in fully supported claim-level judgments.

notes: Hybrid evaluator output: deterministic hard checks plus structured LLM judge evidence. Manual spot checks are still required before claiming product improvement.
